FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data: 10,116 children at baseline (ages 9-10), 7,880 at two-year follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Generic total screen time composite (TV + video games). No smartphone-specific or social media variable. FT-EC2 exclusion: 'Generic total screen time or STS composite with no phone/SM breakdown'

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effects regression: screen time IV with ADHD symptoms DV (β=0.032, p=0.001 at baseline; β=0.030, p=0.001 at follow-up). Mediation analysis with brain structure

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis with cross-sectional and residualized change regression models, mediation analysis

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Translational Psychiatry (2025)

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes cross-sectional analyses with screen time as IV and multiple longitudinal outcomes (ADHD symptoms, brain structure). Screen time is independent variable, not dependent variable. Does not meet FT-EC7 criteria.
notes: Excluded due to FT-IC2 (generic total screen time without phone/SM breakdown). Does not meet smartphone/SM exposure narrowing criteria.

FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract states ABCD study data but no PDF available; cannot confirm U.S. ABCD vs. Dutch ABCD or other cohort from abstract alone

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured: weekday evenings (Cohen's d=0.27) and weekend evenings (Cohen's d=0.35); reported as specific outcome variable associated with chronotype and sleep

FT_IC3.evidence: SEM and t-tests: screen time associated with chronotype, social jet lag, and weekday sleep duration; Cohen's d effect sizes reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Cohen's d values reported for screen time associations (weekday d=0.27, weekend d=0.35); SEM model variance explained (12.9% chronotype, 10.5% social jet lag, 6.2% sleep duration)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional and SEM examining behavioral and psychological predictors of sleep outcomes in Hispanic vs. non-Hispanic adolescents

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Sleep Advances (2025)

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes individual psychological predictors (perceived stress, depression, anxiety) as independent variables alongside demographics, predicting sleep outcomes. Screen time is one of multiple IVs, not the sole dependent variable. Does not meet FT-EC7 criteria.
notes: FT-IC1 marked UNKNOWN due to missing PDF. Cannot definitively confirm U.S. ABCD population. Screen time measure is generic (weekday/weekend) without specific platform breakdown (phone vs. non-phone).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data: N=4,285 analytic sample, baseline ages 9-10 years, data collection 2016-2022, 21 U.S. sites

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures SPECIFIC smartphone and social media addictive use: 6-item Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ), 8-item Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (MPIQ), 6-item Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ). Separate platform-specific scores extracted and analyzed independently

FT_IC3.evidence: Poisson regression and generalized linear models: social media addictive use trajectories associated with suicidal behaviors (RR=2.14, RR=2.39), suicidal ideation (RR=1.46, RR=1.51), internalizing and externalizing symptoms

FT_IC4.evidence: RR=2.39 (95% CI 1.66-3.43) for high-peaking social media; RR=2.14 (95% CI 1.61-2.85) for increasing social media; mean differences for CBCL internalizing/externalizing reported with 95% CIs

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal cohort study with latent class linear mixed models for trajectory identification (years 2-4 follow-up) and outcome associations at year 4

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in JAMA (July 15, 2025, Volume 334, Number 3)

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes individual psychological/clinical predictors (baseline suicidal behaviors, internalizing/externalizing symptoms) as covariates. Addictive use trajectories are independent variables predicting mental health outcomes. Does not meet FT-EC7 criteria (demographic/contextual predictors only).
notes: Strong inclusion. Platform-specific addictive use measures (SMAQ, MPIQ, VGAQ) with separate coefficients extracted and analyzed. Longitudinal trajectory design with prospective associations to suicidality and mental health outcomes.
